output:
Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

Here are the five core questions:

# TEMPER over SHOAL: Exact-Collapse, Tail-Targeted Per-Language-Pair Temperature Recalibration for Cross-Lingual Sentence Embeddings

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Universal cross-lingual sentence embeddings map sentences from many languages into one shared vector space in which semantically equivalent sentences lie close regardless of language. This shared space underpins multilingual document and passage retrieval, multilingual question answering, bitext mining for unsupervised and low-resource machine translation, and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer of downstream classifiers [6,7,8,21]. The central promise is coverage: a single encoder that serves hundreds of languages without a separate per-language pipeline.

Pretrained multilingual language models such as mBERT [4], XLM [22], and XLM-RoBERTa (XLM-R) [3] are the natural backbone, but their off-the-shelf representations are not aligned across languages. Without task finetuning, mean-pooled or CLS embeddings from these models separate into per-language clusters: the dominant axis of variation is language identity, not meaning, so a sentence and its translation can sit far apart while unrelated same-language sentences sit close [20]. Some finetuning step is therefore required to rotate and collapse these clusters into a meaning-aligned space.

The dominant recipe aligns the space with massive parallel supervision. LASER trains a sequence encoder on parallel corpora across dozens of languages [6], and LaBSE [9], building on Artetxe and Schwenk [6,7], finetunes a multilingual transformer with on the order of billions of translation pairs under a translation-ranking objective. These methods produce excellent alignment, but the cost is structural: obtaining clean parallel data at that scale for every language pair is infeasible, and coverage is bottlenecked precisely on the low-resource pairs that need it most.

mSimCSE [2] reduces this parallel-data dependence by extending SimCSE [1] to the multilingual setting and leaning on the strong zero-shot cross-lingual transfer of pretrained LMs [20]. SimCSE is contrastive: it pulls a positive sentence pair together and pushes in-batch negatives apart under an InfoNCE loss [12], L_i = -log( exp(sim(h_i, h_i+)/tau) / sum_j exp(sim(h_i, h_j+)/tau) ), with positives from either unsupervised dropout augmentation or supervised NLI tuples [24,25]. mSimCSE finetunes XLM-R-large with this objective using mostly English-only data and shows the representation transfers cross-lingually; it also studies cross-lingual NLI supervision (e.g., mSimCSE_en,fr and mSimCSE_all over XNLI languages [5]) and small translation-pair finetuning (e.g., mSimCSE_sw). It is evaluated on cross-lingual sentence retrieval (Tatoeba [6], and the BUCC bitext-mining family [8]) and on XNLI [5].

The technical lever this proposal targets is the temperature tau in that InfoNCE loss. Temperature is not a cosmetic constant: it sets a single operating point on the alignment-uniformity trade-off [14] and it controls how sharply the loss weights hard negatives [15], so a small tau sharpens alignment at the cost of uniformity and a large tau does the reverse. In mSimCSE, as in essentially all SimCSE-style training [1,13], tau is a single global scalar, shared across the whole batch and blind to which languages a pair or a negative comes from. Yet the language pair is fully observable, and typology resources -- URIEL and its lang2vec vectors [18] -- expose syntactic, phonological, and geographic distances plus script and family features that make per-pair conditioning a concrete, low-cost option.

This sets up a diagnostic-versus-corrective gap. The field treats temperature as a single hyperparameter, tuned once on a development average and then frozen, and it never conditions that temperature on the language pair being aligned -- even though the difficulty of aligning English to French, to Swahili, and to Tibetan plainly differs. The remainder of this proposal argues that this single global, language-blind calibration is not merely undertuned but representationally incapable of expressing variation that demonstrably exists, that the resulting damage is hidden by the standard macro-averaged metric, and that it can be corrected with a bolt-on that provably reduces to mSimCSE when switched off.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap SHOAL -- Single-Hyperparameter Operating-point Aliasing of Language-pairs. The claim is a representational impossibility, not a tuning complaint. Temperature fixes one point on the alignment-uniformity curve [14]; the alignment-optimal operating point for a cross-lingual pair, tau*(L_i, L_j), is demonstrably non-constant over the language grid, varying with typological/URIEL distance from the anchor, script overlap, and pretraining resource level [18,19]. A single scalar is a zero-dimensional object and cannot represent a non-constant function tau*: L x L -> R+. Consequently the one global tau aliases distinct pairs onto a single compromise operating point, structurally under-aligning the typologically-distant and low-resource tail; the same language-blind setting also leaves cross-lingual false negatives -- an in-batch translation of the anchor counted as a negative -- unsuppressed, and these inflate the InfoNCE denominator unevenly across pairs [16,17].

The damage is silent under the standard dashboard. mSimCSE-style results are reported as macro-averaged Tatoeba/BUCC retrieval accuracy and language-averaged XNLI accuracy [5,6,21], and that average is dominated by near-English, high-resource pairs that the compromise tau serves well (sometimes slightly over-serves). The distant/low-resource tail is a minority of the mass, so its degradation is averaged away and the headline number stays flat while per-pair retrieval on the tail erodes. SHOAL therefore has a precise vanish condition: the harm disappears if and only if the per-pair optimum is uniform -- tau*(L_i, L_j) = tau_0 for all pairs -- and the optimal cross-lingual negative reweighting is the identity. Under exactly that zero condition the single global tau is already optimal and there is nothing to fix.

We close SHOAL with TEMPER -- Typology-Estimated, Mixed-effects Pairwise tEmperature Recalibration -- a thin head bolted onto XLM-R-large and the unchanged mSimCSE InfoNCE objective. For a pair from languages (L_i, L_j), TEMPER predicts a conditional temperature tau(L_i, L_j) = tau_0 * exp(s * g_phi(f_ij)), where f_ij is a frozen feature vector of URIEL/lang2vec syntactic, phonological, and geographic distances plus script-match and resource-bucket indicators [18], and g_phi is a small linear/MLP map gated by a scalar s. To keep the tail estimable, phi is fit by hierarchical partial pooling (a mixed-effects parameterization: a global mean, a typology fixed effect, and language/pair random effects shrunk toward the mean by a learned variance of scale sigma) [26,27], so few- and zero-shot pairs borrow strength from typological neighbors rather than estimating in isolation. In parallel, TEMPER adds language-pair-conditioned false-negative suppression: each cross-lingual in-batch negative receives a weight w_ij in (0, 1] that down-weights its denominator contribution when a cheap pair-conditioned detector flags it as a probable translation of the anchor [7,23]. The encoder, the positive-pair construction, and the optimizer are reused unchanged.

TEMPER is built to vanish on command. At the default s = 0 and w_ij = 1 (equivalently, random-effect variance sigma -> 0 with zero fixed effects), tau(L_i, L_j) = tau_0 * exp(0) = tau_0 for every pair and every negative enters the denominator with weight 1, so the per-example loss becomes -log( exp(sim(h_i, h_i+)/tau_0) / sum_j exp(sim(h_i, h_j+)/tau_0) ) -- the mSimCSE InfoNCE loss at temperature tau_0, term for term. A CI build test instantiates both losses on a fixed reference batch with identical weights and asserts E_collapse = max_batch | L_TEMPER(s=0, w=1) - L_mSimCSE(tau_0) | <= 1e-6 on loss and gradients, failing the build otherwise. This yields a zero-regression guarantee: switching TEMPER off recovers mSimCSE exactly (to floating-point tolerance), and switching it on can only add per-pair corrections the global scalar cannot express. The English-only headline setting is the degenerate boundary where only (en, en) pairs occur at training time, so TEMPER coincides with mSimCSE there by construction.

The novelty is the conjunction of four clauses, no two of which are jointly held by prior work:
- C1. The contrastive temperature is a learned function of inter-language typology, tau(L_i, L_j) from URIEL/lang2vec distances -- not a global scalar, a per-instance hardness scalar, or a single learnable scalar.
- C2. That per-pair calibration is estimated by mixed-effects / hierarchical partial pooling over the language-pair grid, so zero- and few-shot pairs shrink toward typological neighbors.
- C3. False-negative suppression in the cross-lingual InfoNCE denominator is conditioned on the language pair, targeting in-batch translations of the anchor.
- C4. The apparatus carries a CI-gated exact-collapse-to-baseline guarantee (E_collapse <= 1e-6) paired with a tail-targeted, silent-harm evaluation (per-pair metrics stratified by URIEL distance and resource bucket).

## 3. Novel Research Idea and Recommendations (Five-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In mSimCSE's multilingual-supervision regime, can replacing the single global InfoNCE temperature with a typology-conditioned, hierarchically partial-pooled per-language-pair temperature tau(Li,Lj) - plus language-pair-aware false-negative suppression - recover the Tatoeba/XNLI accuracy the macro-average silently sacrifices on typologically-distant and low-resource language pairs, while exact-collapsing to mSimCSE at a zero-gate default (CI-gated E_collapse<=1e-6) for a guaranteed zero regression on all other pairs?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 (INFRASTRUCTURE / broad latent issue). Cross-lingual sentence embeddings are foundational infrastructure: multilingual retrieval, cross-lingual QA, bitext mining that feeds MT training data, and zero-shot transfer all sit on one shared embedding space [6,7,8,9,10,21]. mSimCSE's contrastive-alignment recipe -- English-mostly InfoNCE over XLM-R-large -- is now a dominant, data-light way to build that space [1,2,3]. A structural blind spot in its single-temperature InfoNCE objective is thus a BROAD latent issue, not a niche bug: SHOAL shows one scalar tau fixes exactly ONE operating point on the alignment-uniformity trade-off [14,15] and cannot represent the non-constant optimum tau*(Li,Lj) over the language grid, so every downstream system inherits the same per-pair aliasing.

P2 (SILENT / metric-invisible). The failure never shows on the headline. Tatoeba and XNLI are reported macro-averaged or English-centric [5,6,21], and the typologically-distant/low-resource tail is minority mass, so under-alignment of pairs like en-sw or en-bo is averaged away. Operators read a healthy aggregate while the tail is silently under-served -- precisely SHOAL's "harm is SILENT under macro-averaged metrics" claim. TEMPER's tail-targeted, per-pair evaluation stratified by URIEL distance and resource bucket [18] is what makes the hidden regression measurable at all (clause C4).

P3 (EQUITY / language justice). The harmed languages are not random: they are the typologically-distant, low-resource, under-represented ones [18,19,21] -- exactly the languages multilingual NLP claims to serve. A single global temperature is a STRUCTURAL disadvantage along a language axis: it bakes in a compromise that favors the high-resource, script-sharing, English-near majority and penalizes the tail. Making tau a learned function of typology (C1) and partial-pooling the tail toward its typological neighbors (C2) [18,26,27] targets this inequity directly instead of averaging over it.

P4 (DEPLOYABILITY / zero-regression). TEMPER is a thin score/objective-layer add-on -- a small head g_phi over frozen URIEL features plus a denominator reweighting -- leaving XLM-R-large, the positives, and InfoNCE untouched [2,3,12]. Its provable EXACT collapse (set gate scale s=0 and suppression w_ij=1 => tau(Li,Lj)=tau0 and identity weights => loss and gradients reduce summand-for-summand to mSimCSE at tau0; CI-gated E_collapse <= 1e-6) lets it SHIP IN SHADOW: zero regression on already-served pairs, dial up only on the tail. A correction carrying a formal no-regression guarantee is exactly what is adoptable in production.

P5 (GENERALITY). Typology-conditioned, partial-pooled calibration of a contrastive operating point is a general lens, not an mSimCSE patch. Any encoder trained with a single global InfoNCE temperature over a heterogeneous population of pairs -- multilingual CLIP, multilingual speech/audio encoders, dense multilingual retrievers [12,13,14] -- suffers the same single-operating-point aliasing whenever the optimal temperature varies with a measurable covariate. TEMPER shows how to turn that knob into a shrinkage-regularized function of side information while preserving exact-collapse safety, so it transfers to other mismatch covariates (domain, modality gap, resource level).

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Five structural obstacles. Each is exactly why "just tune tau better" fails, and each is made tractable by a specific TEMPER design choice (C1-C4).

O1 - REPRESENTATIONAL (the binding impossibility): one scalar cannot sit at many per-pair optima. tau in InfoNCE selects ONE operating point on the alignment-uniformity trade-off [14,15]. The per-pair optimum tau*(Li,Lj) is a NON-CONSTANT function over the LxL grid -- it shifts with URIEL/typological distance, script overlap, and pretraining-resource level. A scalar is a 0-dimensional object; a non-constant target tau*: LxL -> R+ cannot be represented by one value. Any chosen value is simultaneously too low for some pairs (over-uniform, breaking alignment on the distant tail) and too high for others. Why naive fails: "grid-search tau" returns ONE compromise minimizing the macro-average (dominated by the high-resource head); it provably cannot cut the per-pair residual on the tail. "Per-pair grid-search" is combinatorial -- for the 36-language Tatoeba subset up to 630 unordered (1260 ordered) pairs, each needing its own sweep -- and starves exactly the low-resource pairs that lack data to tune against. Why tractable: tau*(.,.) is empirically SMOOTH in typology -- neighboring pairs share operating points. A low-capacity g_phi over ~10-20 frozen URIEL/lang2vec features (C1) represents the whole grid with O(10) parameters: an LxL table becomes a smooth regression. We tune the FUNCTION, never per-pair.

O2 - ESTIMABILITY / DATA SPARSITY: the tail has few or ZERO observed pairs. The distant/low-resource tail has very few -- often zero -- observed cross-lingual training pairs (mSimCSE trains mostly on English plus a slice of XNLI-translated NLI and tiny translation sets). A free independent per-pair temperature for an unseen pair is UNIDENTIFIABLE; for sparsely-seen pairs it OVERFITS sampling noise. Why naive fails: an independent per-pair tau has one parameter per pair and no mechanism to set an unobserved pair's parameter; it must fall back to a default or fit noise, and cannot generalize to held-out pairs by construction. Why tractable: TEMPER uses a HIERARCHICAL mixed-effects estimator (C2): global intercept + typology fixed effects + language/pair random effects shrunk by LEARNED variances [26,27]. Partial pooling lets a zero-shot pair INHERIT its operating point from typological neighbors via lang2vec (the fixed-effect prediction), while data-rich pairs get small random-effect corrections. Identifiability comes from the shared typology basis, not per-pair data.

O3 - CONFOUNDING / DISENTANGLING: is the tail bad because of tau, or the encoder? Per-pair retrieval quality is confounded by (a) mis-set tau, (b) weaker XLM-R-large pretraining for tail languages, (c) script/tokenizer FERTILITY inflating sequence length and degrading pooling, (d) noisier tail evaluation/translation data. Attributing tail degradation to temperature vs encoder capacity is genuinely hard; getting it wrong makes TEMPER claim credit it did not earn. Why naive fails: comparing two differently-trained systems changes encoder, data, AND temperature at once; any delta is unattributable. Why tractable: the EXACT-COLLAPSE control arm (C4) is the identification strategy. B1 = TEMPER at s=0, w=1 IS mSimCSE at tau0 -- same encoder, data, seed, weights -- so the ONLY thing varying between B1 and full TEMPER is the temperature function and suppression weights. Every ablation HOLDS THE ENCODER FIXED; the capacity confound is differenced out.

O4 - FALSE-NEGATIVE DETECTION without parallel labels. Language-blind in-batch sampling can place a TRANSLATION of the anchor among the negatives (cross-lingual NLI and translation-pair batches are full of cross-lingual semantic equivalents). InfoNCE then pushes the anchor away from its own translation -- a false negative fighting cross-lingual alignment [16,17]. But deciding WHICH negatives are translations, with no parallel labels at train time, is itself a bitext-mining problem [7]; aggressive suppression can DELETE genuine hard negatives and collapse uniformity. Why naive fails: a single GLOBAL cosine threshold for "is a translation" is wrong because baseline cross-lingual similarity differs by pair (high-resource same-script pairs sit at higher cosine than distant pairs); a global cutoff both misses tail translations and falsely flags head negatives. Why tractable: TEMPER uses a CONSERVATIVE, PAIR-CONDITIONED detector (C3): a margin/ratio score (cosine + the Artetxe-Schwenk margin criterion [7], optionally LASER-margin/SimAlign [23]) with a pair-conditioned threshold, producing a SOFT down-weight w_ij in (0,1] -- never a hard delete. We bound suppressed fraction (<= 5-10%), ablate on/off and strength, and keep strength=0 as the exact-collapse default; uniformity is preserved at low strength.

O5 - OPTIMIZATION COUPLING + COLLAPSE FRAGILITY. Temperature enters InfoNCE nonlinearly -- logits scale by 1/tau, gradients scale roughly as 1/tau, loss is highly sensitive near small tau. A learned per-pair varying tau can destabilize training (exploding/vanishing gradients, oscillation) if it wanders small. Worse, the exact-collapse identity (s=0, w=1 -> mSimCSE) is FRAGILE to floating-point and reduction ordering: adding suppression weights/gate inside the log-sum-exp in a different order than the baseline differs at the 1e-4 level and SILENTLY breaks the collapse guarantee, destroying O3's clean control arm. Why naive fails: learning tau directly (not its log) admits non-positive values; an unclamped per-pair tau invites instability; recomputing the denominator with extra terms "that cancel at default" usually does not cancel bit-for-bit. Why tractable: parameterize tau = tau0 * exp(s * g_phi(f_ij)) -- positivity automatic, s=0 yields tau0 EXACTLY; clamp s*g_phi so tau stays in a safe band (e.g., [tau0/4, 4*tau0]); enforce a CI collapse gate E_collapse <= 1e-6 on BOTH loss and gradients, computed in the same reduction order as the baseline. The fragile identity becomes a checked invariant.

Bottom line: O1 = one scalar cannot encode the grid; O2 = free per-pair tuning cannot fix it (tail unidentifiable); O3 = you must difference out the encoder to even measure the effect; O4 = the negatives themselves are mislabeled and need careful down-weighting; O5 = the fix must not destabilize training or silently break its own safety net. C1-C4 answer each in turn.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The contribution is the JOIN C1 AND C2 AND C3 AND C4. Each prior family lands on at most two clauses (most on zero or one). Clauses: C1 = temperature is a learned FUNCTION of inter-language typology; C2 = that per-pair temperature is estimated by mixed-effects / hierarchical partial pooling over the pair grid; C3 = language-pair-conditioned false-negative suppression in the cross-lingual InfoNCE denominator; C4 = CI-gated exact-collapse-to-baseline (E_collapse<=1e-6) paired with tail-targeted silent-harm evaluation.

Family (i) - Adaptive / learned-temperature contrastive learning. Members: per-instance / hardness-aware temperature, the single learnable CLIP-style scalar, temperature SCHEDULES over training [13,14,15,28]. Lands ADJACENT to C1 (temperature need not be a frozen global constant) but satisfies none of C1-C4 as stated: it is exactly the per-instance/single-scalar/schedule regime C1 explicitly EXCLUDES (no inter-language typology function). No mixed-effects pooling over a pair grid (misses C2), no false-negative suppression (misses C3), no exact-collapse + tail eval (misses C4). COVERAGE: 0-1 of 4.

Family (ii) - Typology / URIEL-informed transfer and language selection. Members: URIEL/lang2vec [18], Choosing Transfer Languages [19], typological priors for source-language ranking and adapter/task selection. Uses the typology SUBSTRATE shared with C1/C2 -- but to PICK source languages or rank transfer candidates, never to set a contrastive temperature. tau is never a function of typology (misses C1), no per-pair temperature pooled by mixed-effects (misses C2), no FN suppression (misses C3), no exact-collapse safety (misses C4). COVERAGE: at most 1 of 4 (the typology input only).

Family (iii) - Debiased / false-negative-robust contrastive learning. Members: Debiased Contrastive Learning [16], Hard Negative Samples [17], in-batch false-negative cancellation. Approximately matches C3 (correcting for false negatives in the denominator) -- but is LANGUAGE-BLIND: it corrects sampling bias generically via a class-prior or hardness term, not conditioned on the language pair / "translation-of-anchor" signal a cheap pair-conditioned detector [23] provides. No typology temperature (misses C1), no mixed-effects pooling (misses C2), no exact-collapse + tail eval (misses C4). COVERAGE: ~1 of 4 (a weakened, language-blind C3).

Family (iv) - Massively-parallel alignment encoders. Members: LaBSE [9], LASER/Tatoeba [6,7], multilingual distillation [10], SBERT-style multilingual encoders [11]. Achieve strong alignment -- but via billions of parallel pairs and a single GLOBAL objective (translation ranking / distillation), i.e. data-HEAVY, not the data-light regime SHOAL/TEMPER target. No per-pair conditional temperature (misses C1), no mixed-effects pooling (misses C2), no conditional FN suppression (misses C3), no exact-collapse-to-mSimCSE (misses C4). Alignment is bought by brute-force supervision, an orthogonal mechanism. COVERAGE: 0 of 4.

Family (v) - Mixed-effects / hierarchical modeling and contrastive-operating-point ANALYSIS. Members: LME "keep it maximal" [26], multilevel/hierarchical models [27], alignment-uniformity and temperature-as-hardness analyses [14,15]. Uses the C2 machinery (partial pooling) and studies the C1 substrate (the contrastive operating point) -- but only for POST-HOC ANALYSIS / explanation, never to CALIBRATE a training-time temperature inside the loss. No typology temperature in the objective (misses C1), no in-loop pooled per-pair temperature (misses C2 as a TRAINING mechanism), no FN suppression (misses C3), no exact-collapse gate (misses C4). COVERAGE: at most 1 of 4 (the statistical tool, applied to analysis not calibration).

BARRIER (one line). The barrier was CONCEPTUAL: temperature was treated as a single global scalar tuned on a dev-set average (a hyperparameter), so the view of tau as a non-constant FUNCTION over the language-pair grid -- estimated by partial pooling and shipped under an exact-collapse safety contract -- was never posed; the ingredients (URIEL, mixed-effects, debiasing) lived in separate literatures and were never joined onto the contrastive temperature knob.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD. Base (unchanged from mSimCSE): XLM-R-large encoder, mean/CLS pooling, InfoNCE objective, SimCSE-style positives. Three supervision regimes as in the source: (i) English-only NLI (SNLI+MNLI entailment positives [24,25]) = degenerate/boundary regime; (ii) cross-lingual NLI (XNLI 15-language MT NLI [5]) = mSimCSE_en,fr / mSimCSE_all; (iii) translation pairs (en-sw from OPUS/Tatoeba) = mSimCSE_sw supervised regime. SHOAL/TEMPER is scoped to (ii)+(iii); English-only is the s=0 boundary. TEMPER additions (only new machinery): (1) Feature map f_ij (frozen, no grad) from URIEL/lang2vec [18] for ordered (Li,Lj): syntactic, phonological, geographic, genetic/family distance; binary script-match; per-language resource buckets (high/mid/low from XLM-R/CommonCrawl token counts) + interaction, ~10-20 dims. (2) Temperature head g_phi: linear or 1-hidden-layer MLP; tau(Li,Lj) = tau0 * exp(s * g_phi(f_ij)); tau0 = source global temperature; s = global gate scale (knob); output clamped so tau in [tau0/4, 4*tau0]. (3) Mixed-effects / partial-pooling for g_phi: g_phi(f_ij) = beta_0 + beta^T f_ij + u_{Li} + u_{Lj} + v_{ij}, random effects u_L ~ N(0, sigma_lang^2), v_{ij} ~ N(0, sigma_pair^2), LEARNED variances; fit jointly with InfoNCE (random effects penalized by a Gaussian prior = L2 with learned precision) or via a light EM/variational shrinkage loop; low-data pairs shrink to the typology fixed-effect mean, zero-shot pairs use the fixed-effect prediction. (4) Pair-conditioned false-negative suppression: for each in-batch cross-lingual negative compute a translation score d_ij in [0,1] from cosine + Artetxe-Schwenk margin/ratio on the CURRENT encoder [7] (optional LASER-margin/SimAlign [23]), pair-conditioned threshold; down-weight in the InfoNCE denominator by w_ij = 1 - sigma_supp * d_ij, sigma_supp in [0,1], w_ij in (0,1]; bound suppressed mass per batch (<= 5-10%). EXACT-COLLAPSE DEFAULT: s=0 and sigma_supp=0 -> tau=tau0 for all pairs and every w_ij=1, so L_TEMPER reduces summand-for-summand to mSimCSE InfoNCE at tau0.

DATASETS. Training: English Wikipedia (~1M sentences, unsup SimCSE dropout positives); English NLI = SNLI [24] + MNLI [25]; XNLI 15-language translated NLI [5] (cross-lingual supervision); small translation pairs en-sw (+ a few low-resource pairs) from OPUS/Tatoeba bitext. Typology features: URIEL/lang2vec [18] (frozen). Evaluation: (1) Tatoeba similarity-search retrieval -- standard LASER 36-language subset [6], extendable to 112+ Tatoeba-Challenge; the TAIL (high-distance, low-resource) is the regime of interest. (2) BUCC bitext mining [8] -- de, fr, ru, zh (F1). (3) XNLI cross-lingual NLI [5] -- 15 languages (accuracy). (4) English STS (STS-B / SemEval STS12-16) -- no-regression. Splits: language-disjoint where applicable; crucially a HELD-OUT-PAIR (zero-shot pair) split -- hold out language pairs entirely from any pair-specific signal to test identifiability (O2).

METRICS. PRIMARY: per-language-pair Tatoeba retrieval accuracy STRATIFIED by URIEL-distance bin and resource bucket; headline = TAIL-PAIR MEAN and WORST-k (k=10). Secondary: BUCC F1; XNLI accuracy; English STS Spearman (no-regression, within epsilon of B1). Fairness/dispersion: max-minus-min accuracy across pairs; TAIL-vs-HEAD gap. Safety/identity: E_collapse <= 1e-6 (loss + gradients) at default. ALSO report macro-average (and English-centric average) to DEMONSTRATE the silent harm: the prediction is that macro-avg stays flat while the tail moves -- the standard reported number could not have revealed SHOAL.

BASELINES. B1 mSimCSE global tau (= TEMPER at s=0, w=1): exact-collapse target / control arm. B2 best single grid-searched tau (strongest scalar ceiling). B3 per-instance / hardness-adaptive temperature (language-BLIND). B4 independent per-pair tau (NO pooling): overfit/instability + held-out failure (O2 negative control). B5 typology-conditioned tau WITHOUT pooling (C1 only): isolates partial pooling. B6 TEMPER full (C1+C2+C3). Context ceilings (NOT to beat -- different data budget): LaBSE [9], LASER [6] (contextualize, not claim to beat).

ABLATIONS. Typology conditioning on/off (B1 vs B5). Pooling: independent (B4) vs no-pooling typology (B5) vs partial-pooling (B6) = the C2 test. FN-suppression on/off and strength sweep (sigma_supp); report suppressed fraction. Feature subsets: syntactic vs phonological vs geographic vs script-match vs resource-bucket only -- which axis carries operating-point signal. Collapse-default verification: assert E_collapse <= 1e-6 in CI.

FALSIFIABLE PREDICTIONS (hypotheses with ranges + refutation; NOT asserted results). P1 (tail gain, head silent): B6 raises TAIL-PAIR MEAN Tatoeba accuracy +3 to +8 points (worst-10 +4 to +12) over B1, while MACRO-average moves < 1.0 point absolute and English STS stays within 0.5 Spearman of B1. Refutation: if macro/STS regress beyond those epsilons, the "silent, free fix" framing fails. P2 (pooling identifiability): on HELD-OUT zero-shot pairs, partial pooling (B6) improves tail retrieval +2 to +6 over B1, while INDEPENDENT per-pair tau (B4) does NOT (-2 to +1, within noise). Refutation (kills C2): if pooling does not beat independent on held-out pairs, partial pooling is unsupported. P3 (false-negative suppression): on SCRIPT-SHARING high-confusability pairs (within-Cyrillic, within-Romance-Latin, zh-ja Han overlap), enabling FN-suppression adds +1 to +4 over TEMPER-without-suppression, suppressed fraction <= 5-10%. Refutation (kills C3): if on == off within noise, or uniformity/STS regress, C3 is unsupported. CI COLLAPSE GATE: E_collapse = max | L_TEMPER(s=0, sigma_supp=0) - L_mSimCSE(tau0) | <= 1e-6 on loss AND gradients; build fails otherwise. Extra refutations: (R-a) if B5 already equals B6 on tail AND held-out pairs, C2 adds nothing; (R-b) if the optimal per-pair tau is empirically CONSTANT across URIEL-distance bins (tau* approx tau0 everywhere), SHOAL VANISHES and the premise is refuted (the spine's own vanishing condition, made measurable).

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (made clear). We expect TEMPER to (a) lift tail-pair and worst-k cross-lingual retrieval on the typologically-distant/low-resource pairs while leaving the macro-average and English STS statistically unchanged -- empirically exhibiting the "silent harm, free fix" signature; (b) show that partial pooling, not independent per-pair tuning, is what makes the gain generalize to held-out zero-shot pairs (the C2 evidence); (c) show that pair-conditioned false-negative suppression adds a further, modest gain concentrated on high-confusability same-script pairs (the C3 evidence); and (d) verify the exact-collapse identity E_collapse <= 1e-6 in CI, so the entire apparatus carries a formal zero-regression guarantee relative to mSimCSE. Together these convert mSimCSE's single global temperature from an undertuned hyperparameter into an estimable, typology-conditioned function with a safety contract.

RESPONSIBLE AI CONSIDERATIONS. Because this work explicitly targets equity for typologically-distant and low-resource languages, we fold in the following safeguards (RAI review verdict: advisory). (1) Data governance: SNLI, MNLI, XNLI, Tatoeba, OPUS, Wikipedia, and URIEL/lang2vec are used under their respective licenses with attribution; mined bitext is treated as noisy and is deduplicated and screened for incidental PII. (2) Dual-use scope: the intended use is research on equitable embedding quality and MT/retrieval data curation; we explicitly discourage use for surveillance or non-consensual scraping of minority-language communities, who have the least data-governance recourse. (3) Typology is a proxy, not ground truth: URIEL/lang2vec distances (including genetic/family features, some of which encode contested, colonial-era classifications) are imperfect engineering features, not claims about languages or their speakers; the method degrades gracefully and does not depend on their correctness -- the partial-pooling design and the R-b vanish test guard against over-trusting typology. (4) Anchor is an artifact, not a center: the English reference frame is inherited from the mSimCSE base and is not normative; tail pairs are reported as first-class headline numbers (tail-mean, worst-k) alongside the macro-average, and gains are framed as reducing a measured language gap, not eliminating inequity. (5) Reproducibility-as-safety: the CI-gated exact-collapse invariant (E_collapse <= 1e-6 on loss and gradients) is a transparency and no-regression contract that makes "ship in shadow" auditable. (6) Contestability and limits: tail metrics are noisier (O3d) and are not deployment guarantees; because retrieval errors can propagate downstream (e.g., wrong pairs mined into MT training), high-stakes use of tail-language outputs should keep a human or native-speaker reviewer in the loop.
